claims.claim_text: Ray Serve is an HTTP serving framework built on Ray for scalable model serving and composition.
claims.span_hint: section: Ray Serve in the Serving Stack
claims.rationale: The Ray Serve index describes Serve as scalable and programmable serving and discusses combining multiple deployments into one application.

claims.claim_text: Ray Serve deployments are Python classes or functions decorated with `@serve.deployment`; replicas are Ray Actors and applications can be called over HTTP or through DeploymentHandles.
claims.span_hint: section: Building a Ray Serve Application
claims.rationale: The Ray Serve key concepts page directly defines deployments, replicas, `@serve.deployment`, applications, HTTP route prefixes, and DeploymentHandles.

claims.claim_text: Ray Serve supports model composition by binding deployments and calling downstream deployments through Python DeploymentHandles.
claims.span_hint: section: Building a Ray Serve Application, paragraph after RAG code
claims.rationale: The model composition guide describes composing deployments with `.bind()` and DeploymentHandle calls inside a Serve application.

claims.claim_text: Ray Serve autoscaling uses ongoing requests per replica, including `target_num_ongoing_requests_per_replica`, `min_replicas`, and `max_replicas`.
claims.span_hint: section: Autoscaling, Batching, and GPU Allocation
claims.rationale: The Ray 2.9 autoscaling guide explicitly defines `target_num_ongoing_requests_per_replica` and the autoscaling replica bounds used in the module.

claims.claim_text: `ray_actor_options` assigns per-replica CPU and GPU resources in Ray Serve, and fractional CPU/GPU values are supported.
claims.span_hint: section: Autoscaling, Batching, and GPU Allocation
claims.rationale: The Ray Serve resource-allocation guide directly documents `ray_actor_options`, `num_gpus`, `num_cpus`, and fractional resources.

claims.claim_text: Ray Serve supports request batching with `@serve.batch`, including `max_batch_size` and `batch_wait_timeout_s`.
claims.span_hint: section: Autoscaling, Batching, and GPU Allocation
claims.rationale: The Ray Serve dynamic batching guide documents the `ray.serve.batch` decorator and the batch size and wait-time parameters.

claims.claim_text: Fractional GPU allocation in Ray should be validated under real workloads because scheduling a fraction of a GPU does not by itself guarantee production performance isolation.
claims.span_hint: section: Autoscaling, Batching, and GPU Allocation
claims.rationale: The current Ray fractional-GPU guidance explicitly tells operators to validate throughput and latency with the actual workload before production.

claims.claim_text: KubeRay provides Kubernetes CRDs including RayCluster, RayJob, RayService, and RayCronJob; RayService combines a RayCluster with Ray Serve deployment graphs.
claims.span_hint: section: Ray Architecture and KubeRay Resources
claims.rationale: The KubeRay getting-started page lists the CRDs and defines RayService as RayCluster plus Ray Serve deployment graphs.

claims.claim_text: RayService uses `serveConfigV2` with fields such as `import_path`, `route_prefix`, `runtime_env`, and deployments, and KubeRay submits that Serve config to the Ray cluster.
claims.span_hint: section: Deploying Ray Serve with RayService
claims.rationale: The RayService guide directly documents `serveConfigV2`, `import_path`, `route_prefix`, runtime environment, deployments, and KubeRay's submission flow.

claims.claim_text: RayService monitors Ray cluster and Serve deployment health and can create a replacement Ray cluster when unhealthy thresholds are exceeded.
claims.span_hint: section: Deploying Ray Serve with RayService
claims.rationale: The RayService guide explains service HA, unhealthy thresholds, replacement clusters, and traffic switching.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes GPU scheduling requires the device plugin path and GPU resource limits such as `nvidia.com/gpu`; Ray actor GPU requests must fit the GPU resources exposed to Ray.
claims.span_hint: section: Installing KubeRay and Deploying a Ray Cluster, GPU worker group paragraphs
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes sources cover device plugins and GPU limits, while the Ray Serve resource guide covers `num_gpus` resource requests for replicas.

claims.claim_text: A Ray cluster has a head node and worker nodes; the head runs cluster-management processes such as GCS/autoscaler, while workers run user task and actor code.
claims.span_hint: section: Ray Architecture and KubeRay Resources
claims.rationale: The Ray cluster key concepts page directly describes head nodes, worker nodes, GCS, autoscaler, and user task/actor execution.

claims.claim_text: The Ray dashboard exposes cluster resources, tasks, actors, Serve applications, logs, and GPU assignment visibility useful for debugging.
claims.span_hint: section: Monitoring and Debugging Ray Serve
claims.rationale: The Ray observability guide documents the dashboard views for tasks, actors, logs, Serve applications, cluster resources, and GPU assignment.

claims.claim_text: Ray originated as a distributed systems project at UC Berkeley's RISELab.
claims.rationale: The claim is real and verifiable through Berkeley RISELab material, but `rise.cs.berkeley.edu` is outside the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Triton Inference Server is an optimized inference serving runtime that supports multiple model frameworks and features such as model repositories and ensembles.
claims.rationale: The Triton upstream repository describes its inference-server role, supported frameworks, model repository workflow, and ensemble/model-configuration features.

claims.claim_text: KServe provides Kubernetes-native model serving through InferenceService resources and commonly integrates with Knative.
claims.rationale: The KServe upstream repository describes KServe as a Kubernetes inference platform and documents InferenceService and Knative installation integration.

claims.claim_text: Seldon Core is a Kubernetes-oriented MLOps/model-serving platform for deploying and managing AI systems, including modular applications.
claims.rationale: The Seldon Core upstream repository describes it as an MLOps and LLMOps framework for deploying, managing, and scaling AI systems in Kubernetes.

further_reading.title: Ray Serve: Scalable and Programmable Serving
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream entry point for Ray Serve concepts, composition, and serving model.

further_reading.title: KubeRay RayService Guide
further_reading.why_relevant: Directly explains how RayService combines RayCluster configuration with Serve application configuration on Kubernetes.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Docs: Schedule GPUs
further_reading.why_relevant: Authoritative reference for the Kubernetes side of GPU allocation used by Ray worker pods.

further_reading.title: Ray: A Distributed Framework for Emerging AI Applications
further_reading.why_relevant: Foundational Ray paper explaining the task and actor abstractions behind Ray Serve.

notes: I treated the scenario, exercises, quiz answers, code blocks, and ASCII diagrams as pedagogical unless they made reusable factual claims. Most citations should attach to the first explanatory occurrence of each Ray/KubeRay/Kubernetes behavior rather than every repeated operational reminder.
